did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and machine learning methods for dermatology classification. Specifically, it employed MobileNet V2 for feature extraction and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classifying skin diseases (Psoriasis, Dermatofibroma, and Melanoma) using the Dermnet dataset. The study also utilized fuzzy c-mean-based segmentation and compared performance against ResNet.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a classification system for psoriasis, dermatofibroma, and melanoma using the Dermnet dataset. It details specific methods including image preprocessing, fuzzy c-mean-based segmentation, MobileNet V2 feature extraction, and SVM classification. The study reports original performance metrics (accuracy 99.12%, precision 98.65%, recall 99.66%) and compares its proposed model against ResNet, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Psoriasis Skin Disease Classification based on Clinical Images
journal: International Journal on Recent and Innovation Trends in Computing and Communication
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-aided diagnosis system for classifying psoriasis, dermatofibroma, and melanoma using clinical images from the DermNet dataset. The methodology involves image preprocessing, fuzzy c-mean-based segmentation for lesion isolation, and feature extraction using a pre-trained MobileNet V2 model. These features are subsequently classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM). The performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. The proposed MobileNet V2 and SVM approach achieved an accuracy of 99.12%, precision of 98.65%, and recall of 99.66%. The results indicate that this method outperforms ResNet and other comparative architectures in terms of accuracy and recall for these skin diseases.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary focus of the study; one of the three classes used in the classification experiment on the DermNet dataset.
One of the three classes used in the classification experiment on the DermNet dataset.
One of the three classes used in the classification experiment on the DermNet dataset. The paper refers to it as a type of skin cancer.
Described in the Introduction section to provide clinical context and contrast with psoriasis; not used as a target class in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: Psoriasis, Dermatofibroma, and Melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin diseases using clinical images from the DermNet dataset. The model uses MobileNet V2 for feature extraction and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification. The task involves distinguishing between three specific disease classes: Psoriasis, Dermatofibroma, and Melanoma. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: A public collection of clinical skin disease images available at http://www.dermnet.com/dermatology. The full dataset contains around 19,500 images in JPEG format with RGB channels covering 23 different skin illnesses. For this study, a subset was used focusing on three specific classes.

use.training: Used to build the intelligent classification tool; the dataset was separated into training and testing sets.
use.testing: Used to test and verify the tool's effectiveness.
use.other: The paper notes that despite working with a 'smaller dataset' (subset of DermNet), the proposed method outperformed others. The full DermNet dataset is described as having ~19,500 images total, but the specific split counts for the subset used in this experiment are not explicitly reported.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with comparative statements made regarding the proposed model's performance against ResNet, but no statistical significance testing was described.

DermNet.notes: The proposed study's accuracy of 99.12% is compared against these baseline results from literature.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNet V2 (used for feature extraction) combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on data from other body parts; transfer learning approach used where parameters from the pre-trained model served as initial parameters for the new model
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (described as 'facial skin image' or 'input skin image' with RGB channels)

feature_extraction_methods: GLCM (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix) for texture features

classification_or_output_head.details: Linear SVM used to replace the softmax classifier. One-versus-all technique used for multi-class classification (Psoriasis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma). Distance metrics used to measure similarity between query image features and database image features.

preprocessing_techniques: Pixel value adjustment to a range of 0 to 1
Resizing images to square measurement if necessary
Scaling images to a size of 224 by 224 pixels
Noise filtering using the image processing toolkit
Contrast stretching (intensity stretching) to improve image contrast
Fuzzy c-mean-based segmentation for lesion isolation
Normalization (mentioned as part of image pre-processing in feature extraction context)
conclusions: The proposed method using MobileNet V2 for feature extraction and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification outperformed ResNet in terms of accuracy (99.12%), precision (98.65%), and recall (99.66%) despite working with a smaller dataset.
The combination of MobileNet V2 and SVM effectively classifies psoriasis, dermatofibroma, and melanoma using clinical images from the DermNet dataset.
Replacing the softmax classifier with a linear SVM minimizes the possibility of overfitting the classification test output.
The MobileNet V2 architecture is suitable for mobile devices and computers with low computing capacity due to its reduced processing requirements compared to classic CNN models.

The study demonstrates that intelligent medical imaging-based diagnosis systems can assist human experts in making better decisions regarding patient health by providing objective inspection and avoiding subjectivity inherent in manual grading.
important_remarks: The study uses a 'smaller dataset' subset of DermNet, but the specific number of images, class distribution, and train/validation/test split sizes are not reported.
MobileNet V2 was pre-trained on 'data from other body parts' rather than standard ImageNet, which may introduce domain bias not explicitly quantified.
The paper claims to have avoided duplication ('without a single instance of duplication') but does not provide evidence of rigorous data leakage prevention or independent external validation.

Performance metrics in Table 2 (e.g., Psoriasis F1-score 98.66%) contradict the Abstract and Conclusion which report an overall accuracy of 99.12% and recall of 99.66%; the relationship between per-class metrics and the overall reported accuracy is unclear.

The study compares its proposed MobileNet V2 + SVM model against other architectures (ResNet, CNN, etc.) using results from cited literature (Refs [16]-[20]) rather than re-running those models on the same dataset, preventing fair direct comparison.
No statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over comparative models.

The dataset source (DermNet) is described as having ~19,500 images total, but the specific subset used for the three classes (Psoriasis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma) is not defined in terms of size or balance.
The paper mentions using a 'new loss function' for the pre-trained MobileNet V2 model but does not specify what this loss function is or how it differs from standard implementations.
